title: Protocols for Structural Typing

glossary.definition: A programming concept where an object's suitability is determined by its methods and properties rather than its explicit class type.
A type system where a variable's type is determined by the methods and properties it has, rather than its explicit class name.

overview.what:
In Python type hints, a Protocol defines a set of methods or attributes that an object must have, without requiring the object to inherit from a base class. It formalizes 'duck typing'. For example, if you define a `Speaker` protocol with a `speak()` method, any class that implements `speak()` is considered a valid `Speaker` by the type checker, even if it doesn't subclass `Speaker`.

The mental model is 'shape-matching': the type checker verifies if an object's 'shape' (its methods and attributes) matches the Protocol's required shape. It's an analogy to checking if a plug fits a socket—the brand of the plug doesn't matter, only the pin layout.
overview.why: Protocols allow you to write strongly typed code without coupling classes together through inheritance, leading to cleaner and more flexible designs.

why_learning_this: You can define what a function needs from an argument without forcing the caller to inherit from a specific base class.
It makes testing easier because you can mock dependencies that match the Protocol instead of mocking complex base classes.

common_mistakes.title: Inheriting from Protocol in the implementation
common_mistakes.explanation: You don't need to inherit from the Protocol in your concrete classes. The type checker matches them structurally. Inheriting defeats the purpose of structural subtyping.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting @runtime_checkable
common_mistakes.explanation: By default, Protocols are only for static type checking. If you want to use isinstance() with a Protocol at runtime, you must decorate the Protocol with @runtime_checkable.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary advantage of using a Protocol over an Abstract Base Class?
recall_questions.answer: Protocols allow type checking based on the shape of an object (its methods and attributes) without requiring the object to explicitly inherit from a base class.

recall_questions.q: How does the type checker verify if an object satisfies a Protocol?
recall_questions.answer: It performs structural subtyping (shape-matching), checking if the object implements all the methods and attributes required by the Protocol.

understanding_checks.question: What does this code print, and why?
understanding_checks.why: The Duck class implements the quack() method, matching the Quacker Protocol. Because Quacker is decorated with @runtime_checkable, isinstance() structurally checks Duck at runtime.

understanding_checks.question: Does a type checker raise an error for passing User() to save_entity()?
understanding_checks.why: User implements the save() method required by the Saver Protocol. Even though User doesn't inherit from Saver, the type checker recognizes it as a valid Saver.

aha_moment.common_guess: False, because Fish does not inherit from Swimmer.
aha_moment.why: It prints True. When a Protocol is decorated with @runtime_checkable, issubclass() structurally checks if Fish has the required methods, bypassing normal inheritance checks.

practice_tasks.title: Define a Protocol for rendering
practice_tasks.prompt: Given this function that expects an object with a render() -> str method, define a Renderer Protocol and use it as the type hint for the obj parameter.

challenge.title: Mocking with Protocols
challenge.prompt: Create a Notifier Protocol with a notify(msg: str) method. Create an EmailNotifier class that implements it. Create a send_alert function taking a Notifier and calling notify('Alert!').
